feed.title: Alex M.
feed.subtitle: Hi there! Welcome to my little corner of the world!

entry.title: Deep Information Propagation
entry.content:
General

The correlation between 2 inputs, $c$, converges to a fixed point $c^$ in depth. Note that $c^ = 1$ is a fixed point.

A fixed point is stable if a perturbation around the fixed point decays. This is equivalent to seeing how the fixed point varies between layers ($\chi_1$) and check whether the value increases $\chi_1 > 1$ or decreases $\chi_1 < 1$.

Asymptotic expansions and depth scales

The distance between the empirical q, c maps and the their fixed points shrinks exponentially in depth, with scales $\xi_c, \xi_q$.

Proof follows expansion around the fixed point i.e. $q_{aa}^l = q^* + \epsilon^l$, similar for $c$. The formulas derived for the depth scales are Eq. 7 and 9.

entry.title: Deep Transformers wihtout Shortcuts - Modifying Self-Attention for Faithful Signal Propagation

link.title: Deep Transformers wihtout Shortcuts - Modifying Self-Attention for Faithful Signal Propagation

entry.content:
Setting

I will not replicate the full setting here, just the necessary notation.

Let $X_l \in \mathbb{R}^{T \times d}$ be the output sequence for block $l$, where $T$ is the number of tokens and $d$ is the embedding size.

Skipless transformer: transformer without skip connection

Vanilla transformer: skipless transformer without normalization

Self-attention

One headed attention mechanism:

\[\begin{align} \text{Attn}(X) &= A(X) V(X) \\ A(X) &= \text{softmax}(\frac{1}{\sqrt{d_k}}Q(X)K(X)^\top) \\ Q(X) &= XW^Q \\ K(X) &= XW^K \\ V(X) &= XW^V \\ \end{align}\]

Causal attention:

\[\begin{align} A(X) = \text{softmax}(M \circ \frac{1}{\sqrt{d_k}}Q(X)K(X)^\top - \Gamma(1 - M)) \end{align}\]

where $M$ is a lower triangular matrix and $\Gamma(1-M)$ is $\infty$ on the upper triangular side.

Constructing trainable deep transformers without shortcuts

For a simplification, consider that we have only self-attention networks:

\[\begin{align} X_l &= A(X_{l-1})V(X_{l-1}) W_l^O \\ &= A_{l-1} V_{l-1} W_l^O \end{align}\]

We have that $X_0$ is the input sequence. Then:

\[\begin{align} X_L &= A_{L}V_{L} W_L^O \\ &= A_{L} X_{L-1} W^V_{L} W_L^O \\ &= \dots \\ &= [A_{L} \dots A_1] X_0 [\prod_{l=1}^{L}W_l^V W_l^O] \end{align}\]

Let $\Pi_l = A_{l} \dots A_1$ and $W_l = \prod_{l=1}^{L}W_l^V W_l^O$. Under the assumption that $W_l^O, W_l^V$ are both orthogonal, we get that $W$ is orthogonal. Then we can compute the kernel matrices at each layer:

\[\begin{align} \Sigma_0 &= X_0 X_0^\top \in \mathbb{R}^{T \times T} \\ \Sigma_l &= X_l X_l^\top \\ &= \Pi_l \Sigma_0 \Pi_l^\top \end{align}\]

The goal is now to control the product $\Pi_l$, which is done in the paper. Quite a nice solution.

entry.title: Fixup Initialization - Residual Learning Without Normalization

link.title: Fixup Initialization - Residual Learning Without Normalization

entry.content:
Introduction

Fixup (fixed-update initialization) was a concurrent work to SkipInit, but with a more complicated initialization scheme. In SkipInit, they scaled the residual branch with a trainable $\alpha$ initially set to $0$. In Fixup, the authors propose a rescaling of the initialization scheme in order to fix the gradient instability.

Authors show that a deep residual network can be trained without batch normalization and achieve same convergence speed and performance.

Quite interesting is that in Section 4.3, the authors achieve good results on remove Layer Norm in Transformers.

ResNets with standard initialization lead to exploding gradients

This is trivially true, see the proof in the SkipInit post, where the authors show that variance of the residual explodes exponentially without using BatchNorm.

Definition: Positively homogenous function of first degree (p.h.)

$f:\R^m \to \R^n$ is p.h. if $\forall x \in \R^m, \forall \alpha > 0$ then $f(\alpha x) = \alpha f(x)$ (basically if you multiply all arguments by a scalar, it’s equivalent to multiplying the function by that scalar).

Definition: Positively homogenous set of first degree (p.h.)

Intuitively, if we have $f$ as a function of parameters $\theta$, then the p.h. set $\theta_{ph}$ is the set of parameters that the p.h. property defined above applies to: $\bar{f}(\theta_{ph}) = f(x; \theta \setminus \theta_{ph}, \alpha \theta_{ph}) = \alpha f(x; \theta \setminus \theta_{ph}, \theta_{ph})$.

For example, ReLU: $max(0, \alpha x) = \alpha max(0, x)$.

Note that a composition of p.h. functions is p.h.

I will skip the notation and only mention it where it is needed.

Assumptions:

1) $f = f_L \circ f_{L-1} \circ \dots \circ f_1$, where each of the $f_l$ is p.h. $\implies$ $f$ is p.h. 2) $\mathbb{E}[W] = 0$ and weights are i.i.d.

Main results

Theorem 1

Let $x_{i-1}$ be the input to the $i$-th block. Then:

\[\begin{align} \| \frac{\partial l}{\partial x_{i-1}} \| \geq \frac{l(z, y) - H(p)}{\| x_{i-1} \|} \end{align}\]

where $p$ is the softmax probabilities and $H(p) = \sum_x -p(x) \log{p(x)}$ is the Shannon entropy.

❗ I’m going to rewrite the proof here because it’s quite simple and interesting, and because I think it is tied to the idea of input output Jacobian. The authors bound the derivative of the loss wrt to the input of the $i$-th layer. However, the loss is just one more operation applied to $x_L$, which means that the Jacobian is “close” to this equation.

Denote by $f_{i \to j} = f_i \circ \dots \circ f_j$. Then $z = f_{i \to L}(x_{i-1})$, where $x_{i-1}$ is the output from the $i-1$-th layer (and the input to the $i$-th layer), and the loss is the Cross Entropy loss.

$f$ is p.h. wrt input

It is easy to see that $f$ is p.h. wrt the input. Since the composition of p.h. functions is p.h., it suffices to show that just one layer of $f$ is p.h. Let $f(x) = max(0, Wx)$. Then, for $\alpha > 0$:

\[\begin{align} f(\alpha x) &= max(0, W \alpha x) \\ &= max(0, \alpha [Wx]) \\ &= \alpha max(0, Wx) & \alpha >0 \\ &= \alpha f(x) \end{align}\]

We have that $z(x_{i-1}) = f_{i \to L} (x_{i-1})$. Then, by the p.h. property, we have that $f_{i \to L}((1+\epsilon)x_{i-1}) = (1 + \epsilon)f_{i \to L}(x_{i-1}), \forall \epsilon > -1$.

Thus, we get at $\epsilon = 0$:

\[\begin{align} \frac{\partial l(f_{i \to L} ((1+\epsilon)x_{i-1}), y)}{\partial \epsilon} &= \frac{\partial l}{\partial z} \frac{\partial z}{\partial \epsilon} \\ &= -y^\top z + p^\top z \\ &= l(z, y) - H(p) \end{align}\]

Let $| \frac{\partial l(x_{i-1}, y)}{\partial x_{i-1}} |$ be the norm of the loss gradient wrt $x_{i-1}$ and $| \frac{\partial l(x_{i-1} + t v, y)}{\partial t} |$ at $t = 0$, be the norm of the directional derivative of the loss in the direction $v = \frac{x_{i-1}}{| x_{i-1} |}$ (definition from [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Directional_derivative#Derivatives_of_scalar_valued_functions_of_vectors]).

Therefore, we obtain:

\[\begin{align} \| \frac{\partial l(x_{i-1}, y)}{\partial x_{i-1}} \| &\geq \| \frac{\partial l(x_{i-1} + t v, y)}{\partial t} \| \\ &= \| \frac{\partial l(x_{i-1} + t \frac{x_{i-1}}{\| x_{i-1} \|}, y)}{\partial t} \| \\ &= \| \frac{\partial l(x_{i-1} + \epsilon x_{i-1}, y)}{\partial t} \| \frac{\partial \epsilon}{\partial t} & \epsilon = \frac{t}{\| x_{i-1} \|} \\ &= \frac{l(z, y) - H(p)}{\| x_{i-1} \|} & \text{Eq. 8} \end{align}\]

This implies that if the loss is large, then the gradients blow up, because $| x_{i-1} |$ is small in lower blocks.

Theorem 2

This theorem bounds $| \frac{\partial l_{avg}}{\partial \theta_{ph}} |$, so the gradient of the loss wrt p.h. sets in the network. Proof is similar to before (see paper).

Intuitively, this theorem says that you might still get blow-up in the earlier layers of the network, since the expected gradient is lower bounded by something that can increase in depth ($\mathbb{E}[\max_{i \in [C]}z_i]$), especially since the logits are unbounded at initialization.

❗ @Amir - these 2 theorems basically quantify how the loss changes wrt the previous layer input, and wrt the weights. Similar to what we discussed regarding Transformers. Maybe we can measure the logits in Transformers at initialization and see if the same analysis applies.

Authors aim to have an initialization such that SGD steps are not influenced by depth i.e. $| \Delta f(x) | = \Theta(\eta)$, where $\eta$ is the learning rate.

Insights

Residual branches update the network in sync

In order to have an $\Theta(\eta)$ (where $\eta$ is the learning rate) step in the network output, we need each of the $L$ residual branches to have an $\Theta(\frac{\eta}{L})$ update on average.

The authors prove that what actually happens at each step is that each layer has a very tiny update, but the different layers are aligned such that the whole network incurs a drastic step in one change. This is true at least in the ReLU MLP case, with residuals (see appendix).

In Theorem 3, the authors show how the network output update $\Delta f(x_0)$ depends on the update on the parameters $\Delta \theta$.

Study of a scalar branch

The authors show how to initialize residual branches such that SGD updates change the network output by $\Theta(\frac{\eta}{L})$ updates in each branch.

Note: The stochastic gradient of a layer is almost orthogonal to its weight. This affects the equilibirium,

They insert multipliers on each residual branch (see [https://arxiv.org/pdf/1706.05350.pdf]).

entry.title: Exponential expressivity in deep neural networks through transient chaos

link.title: Exponential expressivity in deep neural networks through transient chaos

entry.content:
A mean field theory of deep nonlinear signal propagation

Notation:

\[\begin{align} x^l &= \phi(h^l) \\ h^l &= W^l x^{l-1} + b^l \\ \forall l &= 1 \dots D \end{align}\]

where $W_{ij}^l \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \frac{\sigma_w^2}{N_{l-1}})$ and $b^l \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_b^2)$ and $x^0$ is the input layer.

We have that:

\[\begin{align} h_i^l = \sum_{j=1}^{N_{l-1}} W_{ij}^l \phi(h^{l-1}_j) + b_i^l \end{align}\]

By taking $N_{L-1} \to \infty$ and applying CLT, we get that the distribution of $h_i^l$ converges to a Gaussian distribution with $\mu = 0$ and variance $q^l$. To compute the second moment (variance $\sigma^2 := q^l$):

\[\begin{align} q^l &= \frac{1}{N_l} \sum_{i=1}^{N_l} (h_i^l)^2 \\ &= \frac{1}{N_l} [\sum_{j=1}^{N_{l-1}} W_{ij}^l \phi(h^{l-1}_j) + b_i^l] [\sum_{j=1}^{N_{l-1}} W_{ij}^l \phi(h^{l-1}_j) + b_i^l]\\ &= \sigma_w^2\frac{1}{N_{l}} \sum_{j=1}^{N_{l-1}} \phi(h_i^{l-1})^2 + \sigma_b^2 \end{align}\]

Notice that this is a recursive formula. By the law of large numbers, when the number of neurons goes to $\infty$, we get:

\[\begin{align} q^l &= \sigma_w^2 + \mathbb{E}[\phi(x_i^{l-1})^2] + \sigma_b^2 \\ &=\sigma_w^2 + \int \frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi q^{l-1}} \exp{(-\frac{1}{2 q^{l-1}} (x_i^{l-1})^2)}^2} \phi(x_i^{l-1})dx \\ &= \sigma_w^2 \int \mathcal{D}z \phi(\sqrt{q^{l-1} z})^2 + \sigma_b^2 & \text{change } x = \sqrt{q^{l-1}}z \\ &:= \mathcal{V}(q^{l-1} | \sigma_w, \sigma_b) \end{align}\]

For monotonic nonlinearities, $\mathcal{V}$ is mon. increasing, concave, with fixed points $q^*(\sigma_w, \sigma_b)$. Different regimes are discussed in the paper.

Transient chaos in deep networks

In a similar fashion, consider 2 neurons $a$ and $b$, of the same layer: $x^{0,1}, x^{0,2}$. The correlation map across layers of these 2 neurons is:

\[\begin{align} q_{12}^l = \mathcal{C}(c_{12}^{l-1}, q_{11}^{l-1}, q_{22}^{l-1} | \sigma_w, \sigma_c) \end{align}\]

See paper for the individual terms. Also let $c_{12}^l = q_{12}^l(q_{11}^l q_{22}^l)^{-1/2}$ be the correlation coefficient. The correlation coefficeint approaches a fixed point $c^*$ at large depth.

Similarly, there is a recursive formula for $c_{12}^l$. Let $\chi_1 = \frac{\partial c_{12}^l}{c_{12}^{l-1}} _{c=1}$. This quantity refeclts the growth/shrinkage of a random perturbation across one layer.

It is also tied to the Jacobian (see paper). Therefore, if $\chi_1 > 1$ similar points are mapped to different values, and vice versa for $\chi_1 < 1$. Ideally we want $\chi_1 \approx 1$.
entry.summary: notes on Q/C map analysis by Poole et. al

entry.title: Deep Neural Networks as Gaussian Processes

link.title: Deep Neural Networks as Gaussian Processes

entry.content:
Basics

  • the paper derives the equivalence between infinitely wide, deep networks and GPs
  • so far, the equivalence was established only for infinitely wide, 1 layer networks
  • main idea is the CLT

Single layer network

Consider a a single hidden layer neural network, where \(W_{ij}^l \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_w^2/{N_{L-1}})\) and \(b_{i}^l \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_b^2)\).

\[\begin{align} z_i^1(x) &= b_i^1 + \sum_{j=1}^{N_1}W_{ij}^1 x_j^1(x) \\ x_j^1(x) &= \phi(b_j^0 + \sum_{k=1}^{d_in}W_{jk}^0 x_k) \end{align}\]

where the superscript indictates the layer and the subscript indexes in the vector/matrix.

Notice that \(z_i^l\) is a linear combination of normally distributed terms. Taking \(N_1 \to \infty\) and applying CLT, we get that \(z_i^1(x) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu^1, \Sigma^1)\) (note that \(z_i^1(x)\) is defined for only one sample point \(x\)). Since \(z_i^1(x)\) is Gaussian \(\forall x\) in the dataset, we can conclude that the joint distribution over all samples in the dataset is a GP:

\[z_i^1 = (z_i^1(x^\alpha))_{\alpha=1\dots n} \sim \mathcal{GP}(\mu^1, K^1)\]

where

\[\begin{align} K^1(x, x') &= \mathbb{E}[z_i^1(x) z_i^1(x')] \\ &= \mathbb{E}[(b_i^1)^2 + \sum_{j=1}^{N_1} W_{ij}^1 x_j^1(x) \sum_{j=1}^{N_1} W_{ij}^1 x_j^1(x')] \\ &= \sigma_b^2 + \sigma_w^2 \mathbb{E}[x_i^1(x) x_i^1(x')] \\ &= \sigma_b^2 + \sigma_w^2 C(x, x') \end{align}\]

Multiple layer network

The above result generalizes to multiple layer networks. If we take the widith of each layer to infinity \(N_i \to \infty\) in succession, by the same argument as before we get that \(z_i^l(x) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \Sigma^l)\) and \(z_i^l \sim \mathcal{GP}(0, K^l)\), where:

\[\begin{align} K^l(x, x') &= \mathbb{E}[z_i^l(x) z_i^l(x')] \\ &= \sigma_b^2 + \sigma_w^2 \mathbb{E}_{z_i^{l-1}}[\phi(z_i^{l-1}(x)) \phi(z_i^{l-1}(x'))] \\ &= \sigma_b^2 + \sigma_w^2 F_\phi(K^{l-1}(x, x'), K^{l-1}(x, x), K^{l-1}(x', x')) \end{align}\]

Note that the computation is a recurrence relationship which depends on \(K^{l-1}\) via the deterministic function \(F_\phi\), which in turn depends on the nonlinearity. For some nonlinearities, the function can be computed analytically.

Efficiently computing the GP kernel

I will not reproduce it here, but in the paper the authors propose an efficient algorithm to compute the GP kernel, which reduces the complexity. See section 2.5 in the main paper.

Relationship to deep signal propagation

There are several nice insights in this section, tying it to the signal propagation mean-field papers.

It is well known that as the depth \(l \to \infty\), the kernel converges to a constant fixed point \(K^\infty(x, x')\). However, looking at this fixed point is interesting and has been studied.

Plotting for TanH, \(K^\infty(x, x')\) as a function of \(\sigma_w^2, \sigma_b^2\), we observe 2 regions being formed:

  • ordered phase (bias dominates): for different inputs, the features become similar; reason is that all inputs approach the bias vector \(\implies K^l(x, x') \to q^* \forall x, x'\), where \(q^* = q^*(\sigma_w^2, \sigma_b^2)\)
  • chaotic phase (weight dominates): for similar inputs, features become different; \(\implies K^l(x, x) \to q^*\) but \(K^l(x, x') \to q^* c^*\)

We aim for initialization on the boundary of these 2 regions.

Plotting for ReLU, \(K^\infty(x, x')\) as a function of \(\sigma_w^2, \sigma_b^2\), we get \(K^\infty(x, x') = q^* \forall x, x'\) we also observe 2 regions being formed:

  • bounded phase: \(q^*\) is finite
  • unbounded phase: \(q^*\) is unbounded or infinite

In NNGP a similar phenomenon happens. Data flows through the model as long as \(K^L(x, x') - K^\infty(x, x') \neq 0\). This difference shrinks as \(L \to \infty\).

entry.title: Batch Normalization Biases Residual Blocks Towards the Identity Function in Deep Networks

link.title: Batch Normalization Biases Residual Blocks Towards the Identity Function in Deep Networks

entry.content:
Introduction

Before going in depth in this paper, I will note that it is basically the same idea as ReZero: scaling the residual branch at initialization to 0, thus obtaining identity - except they call it SkipInit.

Excerpt from the paper: “SkipInit: Include a learnable scalar multiplier at the end of each residual branch”.

Main insights:

  • Batch norm enables us to train deeper residual networks because it doenscales the residual branch $f^l(x^l)$ by $\frac{1}{\sqrt{l}}$.
  • Batch normalized networks do not benefit from large learning rates when the batch size is small.
  • Batch normalization has a regularizing effect.

An interesting discussion on SkipInit vs orthogonal initialization in TanH nets. Orthogonal initialization does well in TanH networks because TanH is approximately identity around the origin, hence the orthogonal weights only rotate/reflect the input. However, the same initialization does not work with ReLU because ReLU is not identity around the origin.

Why are deep normalized resnets trainable?

Setting and notation

We denote by $x_i^l$, the i-th input to the layer l, $x_i^+ = max(x_i, 0)$ and $\mathcal{B}(\cdot)$ is batch normalization. We assume $W^l_{ij} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 2/\text{fan-in})$. We denote the input to the model $x^1$ and we know that $x^1_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$. Also, we have that $x^{l+1} = x^l + f^l(x^l)$. We define a normalized network as $f^l(x^l) = W^l \mathcal{B}(x^{l+})$ and an unnormalized network as $f^l(x^l) = W^l x^{l+}$

We can easily see the following identities:

\[\begin{align} \mathbb{E}[f_i^l(x^l) | x^l] &= 0 \\ \mathbb{E}[x^{l+1}] &= \mathbb{E}[x^l] + \mathbb{E}[f^l(x^l)] \\ &= \mathbb{E}[x^l] + \mathbb{E} [\mathbb{E}[f^l(x^l) | x^l]] \\ &= \mathbb{E}[x^l] = \dots = \mathbb{E}[x^1] \\ &= 0, \forall l \\ \end{align}\]

Moreover, we have that:

\[\begin{align} Cov(f_i^l(x^l), x^l_i) &= \mathbb{E}[ Cov(f_i^l(x^l), x^l_i | x^l_i)] + Cov(\mathbb{E}[f_i^l(x^l) | x^l_i], \mathbb{E}[x^l_i | x^l_i]) \\ &= 0 + Cov(0, x_i^l) \\ &= 0 \\ Var(x_i^{l+1}) &= Var(x_i^l) + Var(f_i^l(x^l)) \end{align}\]

Before moving forward, assuming $x=max(0, y)$, we write one basic identity regarding ReLU (can be derived by writing down the definition of expectation):

\[\begin{align} \mathbb{E}[x^2] = \frac{1}{2}Var(y) \end{align}\]

Based on the above statements, we can conclude the following:

Unnormalized networks

For the unnormalized type of networks:

\[\begin{align} Var(f_i^l(x^l)) &= Var(x_i^l) \\ \implies Var(x_i^{l+1}) &= 2 Var(x_i^l) = 2^l & \text{Eq. 9} \\ \implies Var(x_i^{l}) &= 2^{l-1} + 2^l \end{align}\]

which means that the variance in depth explodes and that the residuals and the skip connection contribute equally to the output. In order to control for this explosion, we can scale $x^{l+1}$ by a $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ factor.

Normalized networks

For normalized networks: \(\begin{align} Var(f_i^l(x^l)) &= Var(\mathcal{B}(x^l)_i) \approx 1 & \text{large batch} \\ \implies Var(x_i^{l+1}) &\approx Var(x_i^l) + 1 \approx l & \text{Eq. 9} \\ \implies Var(x_i^{l}) &= (l-1) + 1 \end{align}\)

which means that batch norm prevents exponential variance explosion in depth and that the contribution of the skip connection is $l$ times larger than the residual. In conclusion, the output of the skip connection dominates the residuals, biasing the network outputs towards identity. This means that batch norm actually scales $x^{l+1}$ by $\frac{1}{\sqrt{l}}$.

It is important to note that both the forward pass and the backward pass are properly scaled by the batch normalization operation.

entry.title: Wide Neural Networks of Any Depth Evolve as Linear Models Under Gradient Descent

link.title: Wide Neural Networks of Any Depth Evolve as Linear Models Under Gradient Descent

entry.content:
Introduction

In the previous posts, we saw that: 1) NTK - gradient descent is parameter space is equivalent to functional gradient descent in function space, following the Neural Tangent Kernel 2) Under the infinite width limit, both single layer and multilayer networks are equivalent to a GP with a kernel defined using a recursive formula

In this paper, the authors show that for wide neural networks (i.e. infinite width), the models can be linearized into their first-order Taylor expansion, around the parameters $w_0$ at initialization. In addition, they show that gradient-based training with MSE produces test set predictions drawn from a GP with a particular kernel.

Main insight: when width $ o \infty$, models can be replaced by their first-order Taylor expansion, around $ heta_0$ (the params at initialization). This approximation holds well under finite width too.

More concretely, a deep neural network with infinite width can be simplified to its linearized model. Under MSE, the dynamics become an ODE that can be solved in close form.

Theoretical results

Setting

Note the abuse of notation: $ abla_t f := rac{df}{dt} = \dot{f}$. In the paper they use the differential instead of the derivative in writing the statements below (I divided by the learning rate $\eta$, where $\eta o 0$).

Setting is as in the previous posts, weights and biases are Gaussian distributed. The notation is:

\[ egin{align} h^{l+1} &= x^l W^{l+1} + b^{l+1} \ x^{l+1} &= \phi(h^{l+1}) \end{align}\]

with layer width $n_l$. Also define $ heta^l = [W^l, b^l]$ the vector of all parameters for layer $l$ and $ heta$ the vector of all parameters in the network. Denote $f_t(x) = h^{L+1}(x) \in \mathbb{R}^k$, and $l(\hat{y}, y)$ as the loss function. Finally, $\mathcal{L} = \sum_{(x, y) \in \mathcal{D}} l(f_t(x), \hat{y})$.

We can derive the ODE describing the evolution of parameters $ heta$ (also called gradient flow) as:

\[ egin{align} heta_{t+1} &= heta_t - \eta abla_ heta \mathcal{L} \ &= heta_t - \eta abla_ heta f_t(\mathcal{X})^ op abla_{f_t(\mathcal{X})}\mathcal{L} & ext{chain rule}\ \implies rac{ heta_{t+1} - heta_{t}}{\eta} &= - abla_ heta f_t(\mathcal{X})^ op abla_{f_t(\mathcal{X})}\mathcal{L} \ \implies extcolor{red}{\dot{ heta_t}} &= - abla_ heta f_t(\mathcal{X})^ op abla_{f_t(\mathcal{X})}\mathcal{L} & \eta o 0 \end{align}\]

Similarly, we can derive the evolution of the logits $f_t$:

\[ egin{align} abla_t f_t(\mathcal{X}) &= abla_ heta f_t(\mathcal{X}) extcolor{red}{ abla_t heta} & ext{chain rule} \ &= -\underbrace{ abla_ heta f_t(\mathcal{X}) abla_ heta f_t(\mathcal{X})^ op}_{\Theta_t(\mathcal{X}, \mathcal{X})} abla_{f_t(\mathcal{X})}\mathcal{L} \ \end{align}\]

where we refer to $\hat{\Theta}_t$ as the empirical tangent kernel.

Linearized networks

We replace the outputs of the neural network with their Taylor expansion:

\( egin{align} f_t^{lin}(x) = f_0(x) + abla_ heta f_0(x)|_{ heta = heta_0} \underbrace{( heta_t - heta_0)}_{\omega_t} \end{align}\) where $f_0$ is the initial output of the network.

Plugging into the above derivation of gradient flow, we get:

\[ egin{align} \dot{\omega}_t &= abla_ heta f_0(\mathcal{X})^ op abla_{f_t^{lin}(\mathcal{X})}\mathcal{L} \ \dot{f_t}^{lin}(x) &= \hat{\Theta}_0({x, \mathcal{X}}) abla_{f_t^{lin}(\mathcal{X})}\mathcal{L} \ \end{align}\]

Under the MSE loss, these ODEs have clsoed form solutions (see section 2.2 in the paper). Therefore, without doing GD steps, we can compute the time evolution of the weights and the logits in a linearized neural network.

Taking the width to infinity in each layer yields a Gaussian Process, with a certain mean and covariance described by a kernel (see form in paper, eq 13).

GPs from GD training

TODO (I don’t understand the details in this section too well) The main insight here is that $ orall x \in \mathcal{X}_{test}$, $f_t^{lin}(x)$ converges to a Gaussian distribution when taking the width $ o \infty$.

Infinite width networks are linearized networks

The authors show that applying GD with learning rate $\eta < \eta_{critical}$ and taking the width of all layers $ o \infty$, then $f_t^{lin}(x) o \mathcal{N}(\mu(\mathcal{X}_T), \Sigma(\mathcal{X}_T, \mathcal{X}_T))$ (see form of mean and covariance in paper, eq. 15).

entry.title: Neural Tangent Kernel
entry.content:
Functional Gradient Descent

For examples and plots see here.

Suppose we want to learn a function \(f(x)\) using gradient descent. One example would be to parameterize \(f\) as a linear function using weights \(w\): \(f(x) = w^ op x\).

In order to learn \(w\) we take a loss function, i.e. MSE:

\( egin{align} L(w) &= \sum_{i=1}^n (y_i - w^ op x_i)^2 + \| w \|^2 \ abla L(w) &= \sum_{i=1}^n -2 x_i (y_i - w^ op x_i) + 2w \ &= \sum_{i=1}^n (-2x_iy_i + 2w^ op \| x_i \|^2) + 2w \end{align}\).

Then we do gradient descent steps on $w$:

\[w = w - \alpha abla L(w)\]

Using functional gradient descent, this can be generalized to any function \(f\):

\[ egin{align} L(f) &= \sum_{i=1}^n (y_i - f(x_i)) + \| f \|^2 \ f_{t+1} &= f_t - \alpha abla L(f) \end{align}\]

This has 2 advantages:

  • Some loss functions are non-convex in parameter space but can be convex in functional space
  • In NTK, when width \( ightarrow \infty\), the weights become almost static between GD steps; however we can still study the function trajectory during GD in functional space

Functionals

Some basic functional notions are needed to understand this post.

Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space (RKHS)

Denote RKHS of (fixed) kernel \(k\) by \(\mathcal{H_k}\), \(k(\cdot, \cdot)\) is a kernel function and \(K_{ij} = k(x_i, x_j)\). Then:

\[f \in \mathcal{H_k} \implies f(\cdot) = \sum_{i=1}^n eta_i k(x_i, \cdot), eta_i \in \mathbb{R}\]

In other words, \(f\) is in RKHS if it can be written as a weighted sum of kernel functions evaluated over \(n\) points. Note that \(f\) is completely determined by the \( eta_i\) and \(x_i\).

Inner product and norm

Let \(f, g \in \mathcal{H_k}\). Then:

\[ egin{align} f \cdot g &= \sum_{i=1}^{n_f} \sum_{j=1}^{n_g} \alpha_i eta_j k(x_i, x_j) = \alpha K^ op eta \ \| f \|^2 &= f \cdot f = \alpha K^ op eta \end{align}\]

Reproducing property

\[f \cdot k(x, \cdot) = \sum_{i=1}^n eta_i [k(x_i, \cdot) k(x, \cdot)] = \sum_{i=1}^n eta_i k(x_i, x) = f\]

Evaluation functional

\[E_x[f] = f(x)\]

Functional derivative

From the definition of the derivative, the functional derivative is the coefficient of the linear term in the Taylor expansion of the functional.

Example 1 - for the functional \(E_x[f] = f(x)\):

\[ egin{align} E_x[f + df] &= f(x) + df(x) \ &= E_x[f] + df(x) \ &= E_x[f] + k(x, \cdot) \cdot df \ &\implies abla E_x[f] = k(x, \cdot) \end{align}\]

Example 2 - for the functional \(E[f] = \| f \|^2\)

\[ egin{align} E[f + df] &= (f + df) (f + df) \ &= \| f \| + 2 f \cdot df + \| df \|^2 \ &= E[f] + 2 f \cdot df + \| df \|^2 \ &\implies abla E[f] = 2f \end{align}\]

Chain rule

Let \(E[f]\) be a functional and \(g : \mathbb{R} o \mathbb{R}\). Then:

\[ abla g(E[f]) = abla E[f] g'(E[f])\]

Gaussian Processes (GP)

This is a (very) short and handwavy recap on GPs, but it suffices to show the relevant connections to NTK.

Let \(Y \sim \mathcal{N(0, \Sigma_y)}\) be the training set and \(X \sim \mathcal{N(0, \Sigma_x)}\) be the test set (we can always substract the mean to center them in 0). We setup the prior distribution \(P_X\) such that \(\Sigma_x^{ij} = K(X_i, X_j)\). We also have the joint distribution

\[P_{X, Y} = egin{bmatrix} X \ Y \end{bmatrix} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, egin{bmatrix} \Sigma_{xx} & \Sigma_{xy} \ \Sigma_{yx} & \Sigma_{yy}\end{bmatrix})\]

where each covariance is setup similarly via the kernel \(K\). Then, due to the property that the Normal distribution is closed under conditioning and marginalization, we can compute the posterior

\[P_{X|Y}\]

From the posterior we can sample multiple functions that fit our training data.

Neural Tangent Kernel

  • We know that at initialization, when taking width \( o \infty\), ANNs behave like GPs
  • NTK proves that this is also true during training, where the GP kernel used in the training is the “Neural Tangent Kernel”
  • The NTK stays constant during training when taking width \( o \infty\)

In the following derivations, I will not use biases.

Setting

The following setting is used in the paper:

\( egin{align} f_ heta(x) &= ilde{\alpha}^{(L)}(x) \ \alpha^{(0)}(x) &= x \ ilde{\alpha}^{(l+1)}(x) &= rac{1}{\sqrt{n_l}} W^{(l)} \alpha^{(l)}(x) \ \alpha^{(l)} &= \sigma( ilde{\alpha}^{(l)}(x)) \end{align}\) where \(W_{ij}^{(l)} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, rac{1}{n_l})\).

Define the bilinear form:

\[\langle f, g angle_{p^{in}} = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p^{in}}[ f(x)^ op g(x)]\]

where \(p^{in}\) is the distribution of the input set (assume empirical distribution over \(N\) points). Similarly,

\[\langle f, g angle_{K} = \mathbb{E}_{x, x' \sim p^{in}}[ f(x)^ op K(x, x') g(x')]\]

.

Also, let \(F^{(L)}: \mathbb{R}^P o \mathcal{F}\) be the realization function, which maps parameters \( heta\) to a function \(f_ heta\) (basically takes in parameters and returns a function parameterized by these parameters), and \( abla_{W_{ij}^{(l)}}F^{(L)}\) be the derivative of the realization function w.r.t. the weights. Define also \(\mu : \mathcal{F} o \mathbb{R}, \mu = \langle d, \cdot angle_{p^{in}}, d \in \mathcal{F}\). Plugging in \(d = K_{i, \cdot}(x, \cdot)\) in the previous definition (since \(K_{i, \cdot}(x, \cdot) \in \mathcal{F}\)), we get:

\[ egin{align} f_{\mu, i}(x) = \langle d, K_{i, \cdot}(x, \cdot) angle \end{align}\]

Instead of doing gradient descent on the parameters \( heta\) (which we will see stay almost constant during training as the width \( o \infty\)), we do functional gradient descent on the function \(f_ heta\) itself, using a cost \(C : \mathcal{F} o \mathbb{R}\).

Define the functional derivative of \(C\) at a point

\(f_0 \in \mathcal{F}\) as \( abla_fC|_{f_0}\)

and the dual

\[d|_{f_0} \in \mathcal{F}\]

such that

\[ abla_fC|_{f_0} = \langle d|_{f_0}, \cdot angle_{p^{in}}\]

.

Random functions approximation

Before moving onto ANNs, this is a simplification in which the realization function is linear.

Let \(P\) random functions \(f^{(p)}\) define a random linear parametrization:

\[ egin{align} F^{lin} = f_ heta^{lin} = rac{1}{\sqrt{P}} \sum_{p=1}^P heta_p f^{(p)} \end{align}\]

The partial derivatives are then:

\[ egin{align} abla_{ heta_p} F^{lin}( heta) &= rac{1}{\sqrt{P}} f^{(p)} \ abla_{t} F^{lin}( heta(t)) &= rac{1}{\sqrt{P}} \sum_{p=1}^P abla_t heta_p(t) f^{(p)} \ \end{align}\]

Writing down the gradient descent step on the parameters:

\[ egin{align} heta_p(t+dt) &= heta_p(t) - dt abla_{ heta_p}(C \circ F^{lin})|_{ heta(t)} \ \implies abla_t heta_p(t) &= - abla_{ heta_p}(C \circ F^{lin})|_{ heta(t)} \ &= - abla_{ heta_p}F^{lin} abla_{F^{lin}}C|_{f_{ heta(t)}^{lin}} \ &= -( rac{1}{\sqrt{P}} f^{(p)}) abla_{F^{lin}}C|_{f_{ heta(t)}^{lin}} \ &= - rac{1}{\sqrt{P}} \langle d|_{f_{ heta(t)}^{lin}}, f^{(p)} angle_{p^{in}} \end{align}\]

Plugging in the above 2 equations we get the evolution of the function \(f_{ heta(t)}^{lin}\) in function space through GD:

\[ egin{align} abla_{t} f^{lin}_{ heta(t)} &= - rac{1}{P} \sum_{p=1}^P \langle d|_{f_{ heta(t)}^{lin}}, f^{(p)} angle_{p^{in}} f^{(p)} \ &= - abla_{ ilde{K}}C|_{f_ heta^{lin}} \end{align}\]

where \( ilde{K}\) is the tangent kernel (by the definition in the paper in section 3).

In conclusion, parameter gradient descent on \(C \circ F^{lin}\) is equivalent to functional gradient descent in the function space with the tangent kernel \( ilde{K}\).

NTK

Similar to above, in ANNs, the network function evolution is:

\[ egin{align} abla_t f_{ heta(t)} &= - abla_{\Theta^{(L)}C|_{f_{ heta(t)}}} \ \Theta^{L}( heta) &= \sum_{p=1}^P abla_{ heta_p}F^{(L)}( heta) \otimes abla_{ heta_p}F^{(L)}( heta) \end{align}\]

where \(\Theta^{L}( heta)\) is the NTK.

For the following statements, check proof in the paper.

At initialization

Recall that \(f_ heta(x)\) is the preactivation of the final layer in an \(L\) layer deep ANN and \(f_{ heta, k}(x), k=1,\dots,n_L\) are the neurons in the final layer preactivation. At initialization, when taking the width of each layer to infinity, i.e. \(n_1, \dots n_{L-1} o \infty\), the neurons \(f_{ heta,k}\) converge to GPs, with covariance \(\Sigma_L\), defined recursively by:

\[ egin{align} \Sigma^{(1)}(x, x') &= rac{1}{n_0}x^ op x' \ \Sigma^{(L+1)}(x, x') &= \mathbb{E}_{f \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \Sigma^{(L)})}[\sigma(f(x))\sigma(f(x'))] \end{align}\]

Note that, the above result showed that each of the output neurons converges to a GP, each with its own covariance matrix. However, the stronger result showed in the paper is that under the same conditions and the same limit, all of the covariance matrices converge to a deterministic NTK kernel \(\Theta^{(L)}\) (see definition in the paper under Theorem 1).

During training

It is also true that the NTK stays asymptotically constant during training, under the infinite width regime. Note that, during the infinite width regime, while the individual variation of each weight entry is small, the total variation is large, allowing the lower layers to learn.
entry.summary: notes on NTK and related material

entry.title: reading list
entry.content:
Side note

❌ Reminder to myself: these notes should not, under any circumstance, be copy-pasted into the thesis/paper, without rewriting them. These are simply study notes and the wording is not original, hence copy-pasting is considered plagiarism. ❌

Papers marked with ✅, are the ones I’ve read in depth (and preferably took notes on).

There is no particular order to these sections.

Training without normalization

  • ✅ Batch Normalization Biases Residual Blocks Towards the Identity Function in Deep Networks
  • ✅ Fixup Initialization: Residual Learning Without Normalization

NTK

  • ✅ Neural Tangent Kernel: Convergence and Generalization in Neural Networks
  • ✅ Deep Neural Networks as Gaussian Processes
  • ✅ Wide Neural Networks of Any Depth Evolve as Linear Models Under Gradient Descent
  • Tensor Programs II: Neural Tangent Kernel for Any Architecture

Mean Field, Dynamical Isometry and designing inits

  • ✅ Exponential expressivity in deep neural networks through transient chaos
  • Deep Information Propagation
  • Resurrecting the sigmoid in deep learning through dynamical isometry: theory and practice
  • Dynamical Isometry and a Mean Field Theory of CNNs: How to Train 10,000-Layer Vanilla Convolutional Neural Networks
  • Dynamical Isometry and a Mean Field Theory of RNNs: Gating Enables Signal Propagation in Recurrent Neural Networks
  • Dynamical Isometry and a Mean Field Theory of LSTMs and GRUs
  • The Emergence of Spectral Universality in Deep Networks
  • Mean Field Residual Networks: On the Edge of Chaos
  • Exact solutions to the nonlinear dynamics of learning in deep linear neural networks.

Transformers

  • Rapid training of deep neural networks without skip connections or normalization layers using Deep Kernel Shaping
  • Deep Learning without Shortcuts: Shaping the Kernel with Tailored Rectifiers
  • ✅ Deep Transformers without Shortcuts: Modifying Self-attention for Faithful Signal Propagation
  • [https://transformer-circuits.pub/2021/framework/index.html]
  • Infinite attention: NNGP and NTK for deep attention networks
  • [https://hyunjik11.github.io/talks/Attention_the_Analogue_of_Kernels_in_Deep_Learning.pdf]

Rank collapse

  • ✅ Attention is not all you need: pure attention loses rank doubly exponentially with depth
  • ✅ Signal Propagation in Transformers: Theoretical Perspectives and the Role of Rank Collapse
  • ✅ ReZero is All You Need: Fast Convergence at Large Depth

Misc

  • $L_2$ L2 Regularization versus Batch and Weight Normalization
entry.summary: papers relevant for my msc thesis
